name: Harpy
description: Harpies are monstrous, winged creatures in Greek mythology, often depicted as having the body of a bird and the face of a woman. They are known for their insatiable hunger and their habit of snatching food and defiling it. Initially, they were considered wind spirits, but later evolved into symbols of punishment and torment. Described as vicious and greedy, they are frequently associated with foul smells and uncleanness. Harpies are often portrayed as agents of the gods, particularly Zeus, tasked with carrying out divine retribution. They are known for their piercing cries and their ability to disappear suddenly. Their unpleasant nature and scavenging habits made them figures of fear and disgust in ancient Greek culture. They are also sometimes linked to storms and the destructive power of nature. The Harpies' role in mythology highlights the ancient Greeks' anxieties about hunger, pollution, and divine punishment. Their image has evolved over time, but their core characteristics of greed, filth, and winged flight remain consistent. They are a lasting symbol of the darker aspects of the natural world and the potential for monstrous behavior.
subtitle: Winged monsters of wind and greed
appearance: Harpies are typically depicted as having the body of a bird, often a vulture or eagle, with the face and breasts of a woman. They have long, sharp claws and their wings are large and powerful. Their faces are usually pale and gaunt, reflecting their insatiable hunger. They are often described as being covered in filth and emitting a foul odor.
habitat: Harpies are said to inhabit remote and desolate places, such as cliffs, caves, and islands. They are often associated with stormy weather and areas where food is plentiful, allowing them to easily steal and defile it.
behavior: Harpies are known for their greedy and destructive behavior. They swoop down to steal food from unsuspecting victims, often defiling what they cannot carry away. They are also said to be fond of tormenting humans with their piercing cries and unpredictable attacks. They are generally unpleasant and aggressive creatures.

keyMyths.title: Phineus and the Harpies
keyMyths.description: King Phineus was tormented by Harpies who stole or defiled his food whenever he tried to eat. The Argonauts, Zetes and Calais, pursued the Harpies and drove them away, although they were spared death by Iris, the messenger of the gods, who promised they would no longer bother Phineus.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Mentions the Harpies as daughters of Thaumas and Electra.
sources.text: Mentions the Harpies as daughters of Thaumas and Electra.

sources.author: Virgil
sources.description: Depicts the Harpies as foul and dangerous creatures who defile food.
sources.text: Depicts the Harpies as foul and dangerous creatures who defile food.
